    PyTao Basics¶

    Inititialize Tao¶

    Tao is the basic object. Anything used to initialize Tao on the command line can be used to initialize a Tao object

    In [1]:
    Copied! 
    from pytao import Tao
    from pytao import Tao
    In [2]:
    Copied! 
    tao = Tao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init", noplot=True)
# Equivalent to: 
#   tao = Tao("-init $ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init -noplot")
    tao = Tao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init", noplot=True) # Equivalent to: # tao = Tao("-init $ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init -noplot")

    Send a command¶

    Anything that you would normally type at a Tao> prompt can be sent as a string. The return is a list of output strings. To send a command:

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("show lat 1:10")
    tao.cmd("show lat 1:10")
    Out[3]:
    ['# Values shown are for the Downstream End of each Element (Girder elements shown at ref point):',
 '# Index  name          key                       s       l    beta   phi_a    eta   orbit    beta   phi_b    eta   orbit  Track',
 '#                                                                a   [2pi]      x  x [mm]       b   [2pi]      y  y [mm]  State',
 '      1  IP_L0         Marker                0.000   0.000    0.95   0.000  -0.00  -0.017    0.02   0.000   0.00   0.001  Alive',
 '      2  CLEO_SOL#3    Solenoid              0.622   0.622    1.34   0.093  -0.02   1.470   21.81   0.244   0.00   0.041  Alive',
 '      3  DET_00W       Marker                0.622   0.000    1.34   0.093  -0.02   1.470   21.81   0.244   0.00   0.041  Alive',
 '      4  CLEO_SOL#4    Solenoid              0.638   0.016    1.36   0.094  -0.02   1.507   22.92   0.244   0.00   0.043  Alive',
 '      5  Q00W\\CLEO_SOL Sol_Quad              1.755   1.117    7.73   0.160  -0.09   5.505   88.01   0.247  -0.01   0.486  Alive',
 '      6  Q00W#1        Quadrupole            2.163   0.408   15.96   0.166  -0.13   8.151   76.38   0.248  -0.01   0.717  Alive',
 '      7  D003          Drift                 2.493   0.331   27.02   0.169  -0.17  10.705   60.25   0.249  -0.02   0.931  Alive',
 '      8  DET_01W       Marker                2.493   0.000   27.02   0.169  -0.17  10.705   60.25   0.249  -0.02   0.931  Alive',
 '      9  D004          Drift                 2.924   0.431   45.79   0.171  -0.22  14.030   42.12   0.250  -0.02   1.209  Alive',
 '     10  Q01W          Quadrupole            3.874   0.950   66.94   0.173  -0.26  16.851   28.95   0.255  -0.02   1.213  Alive',
 '# Index  name          key                       s       l    beta   phi_a    eta   orbit    beta   phi_b    eta   orbit  Track',
 '#                                                                a   [2pi]      x  x [mm]       b   [2pi]      y  y [mm]  State',
 '# Values shown are for the Downstream End of each Element (Girder elements shown at ref point):']

    Send a list of commands. This returns the corresponding list of outputs:

    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmds(["set lattice model=design", "set ele Q00W x_offset = 1e-6"])
    tao.cmds(["set lattice model=design", "set ele Q00W x_offset = 1e-6"])
    Out[4]:
    [[], []]

    Jupyter magic %%tao¶

    This is an alternative way to send commands to Tao directly in the jupyter notebook, using the %%tao magic. Multiple lines can be executed.

    In [5]:
    Copied! 
    %%tao
sho lat 1:10
sho ele 4
    %%tao sho lat 1:10 sho ele 4
    -----------------
Tao> sho lat 1:10
# Values shown are for the Downstream End of each Element (Girder elements shown at ref point):
# Index  name          key                       s       l    beta   phi_a    eta   orbit    beta   phi_b    eta   orbit  Track
#                                                                a   [2pi]      x  x [mm]       b   [2pi]      y  y [mm]  State
      1  IP_L0         Marker                0.000   0.000    0.95   0.000  -0.00  -0.018    0.02   0.000   0.00   0.001  Alive
      2  CLEO_SOL#3    Solenoid              0.622   0.622    1.34   0.093  -0.02   1.469   21.81   0.244   0.00   0.042  Alive
      3  DET_00W       Marker                0.622   0.000    1.34   0.093  -0.02   1.469   21.81   0.244   0.00   0.042  Alive
      4  CLEO_SOL#4    Solenoid              0.638   0.016    1.36   0.094  -0.02   1.507   22.92   0.244   0.00   0.044  Alive
      5  Q00W\CLEO_SOL Sol_Quad              1.755   1.117    7.73   0.160  -0.09   5.505   88.01   0.247  -0.01   0.487  Alive
      6  Q00W#1        Quadrupole            2.163   0.408   15.96   0.166  -0.13   8.151   76.38   0.248  -0.01   0.719  Alive
      7  D003          Drift                 2.493   0.331   27.02   0.169  -0.17  10.705   60.25   0.249  -0.02   0.932  Alive
      8  DET_01W       Marker                2.493   0.000   27.02   0.169  -0.17  10.705   60.25   0.249  -0.02   0.932  Alive
      9  D004          Drift                 2.924   0.431   45.79   0.171  -0.22  14.030   42.12   0.250  -0.02   1.210  Alive
     10  Q01W          Quadrupole            3.874   0.950   66.94   0.173  -0.26  16.851   28.95   0.255  -0.02   1.213  Alive
# Index  name          key                       s       l    beta   phi_a    eta   orbit    beta   phi_b    eta   orbit  Track
#                                                                a   [2pi]      x  x [mm]       b   [2pi]      y  y [mm]  State
# Values shown are for the Downstream End of each Element (Girder elements shown at ref point):
--------------
Tao> sho ele 4
Element # 4
Element Name: CLEO_SOL#4
Key: Solenoid
S_start, S:      0.622301,      0.637956
Ref_time_start, Ref_time:  2.075773E-09,  2.127992E-09

Attribute values [Only non-zero values shown]:
    1  L                           =  1.5655000E-02 m        31  L_SOFT_EDGE                 =  0.0000000E+00 m
   10  FRINGE_TYPE                 =  None (1)               11  FRINGE_AT                   =  No_End (4)
   13  SPIN_FRINGE_ON              =  T (1)
   18  KS                          = -8.5023386E-02 1/m      49  BS_FIELD                    =  1.5000000E+00 T
   47  PTC_CANONICAL_COORDS        =  T (1)
   53  P0C                         =  5.2890000E+09 eV           BETA                        =  1.0000000E+00

       TRACKING_METHOD              =  Bmad_Standard             APERTURE_AT                =  No_Aperture

    PyTao element helpers¶

    For convenience, you may access element information through tao.ele (for a single element) or tao.eles (for one or more elements).

    In [6]:
    Copied! 
    ele1 = tao.ele("1")
    ele1 = tao.ele("1")
    In [7]:
    Copied! 
    (ele1.name,
 ele1.head.key,
 ele1.head.ix_ele, 
 ele1.head.ix_branch,
 ele1.floor.beginning.actual.x,
 ele1.floor.end.actual.x,
)
    (ele1.name, ele1.head.key, ele1.head.ix_ele, ele1.head.ix_branch, ele1.floor.beginning.actual.x, ele1.floor.end.actual.x, )
    Out[7]:
    ('IP_L0', 'Marker', 1, 0, 0.0, 0.0)

    There is a lot of information contained in this object, so that you don't have to query each bit yourself:

    In [8]:
    Copied! 
    ele1.floor
    ele1.floor
    Out[8]:
    ElementFloorAll(
    which='model',
    beginning=ElementFloor(
        which='model',
        where='beginning',
        actual=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
        ),
        reference=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[ 1., -0.,  0.],
       [ 0.,  1.,  0.],
       [-0.,  0.,  1.]])
        ),
        slaves={}
    ),
    center=ElementFloor(
        which='model',
        where='center',
        actual=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
        ),
        reference=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[ 1., -0.,  0.],
       [ 0.,  1.,  0.],
       [-0.,  0.,  1.]])
        ),
        slaves={}
    ),
    end=ElementFloor(
        which='model',
        where='end',
        actual=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
        ),
        reference=ElementFloorPosition(
            x=0.0,
            y=0.0,
            z=0.0,
            theta=0.0,
            phi=0.0,
            psi=0.0,
            wmat=array([[ 1., -0.,  0.],
       [ 0.,  1.,  0.],
       [-0.,  0.,  1.]])
        ),
        slaves={}
    )
)
    In [9]:
    Copied! 
    list(type(ele1).model_fields)
    list(type(ele1).model_fields)
    Out[9]:
    ['ele_id',
 'which',
 'head',
 'attrs',
 'bunch_params',
 'chamber_walls',
 'comb',
 'control_vars',
 'floor',
 'grid_field',
 'lord_slave',
 'mat6',
 'multipoles',
 'orbit',
 'photon',
 'twiss',
 'wake',
 'wall3d']

    The choice of these fields to fill is configurable with Tao.ele() and Tao.eles(). See its help for more information.

    For more details and examples, see elements.ipynb.

    In [10]:
    Copied! 
    # Access general attributes which are element-specific:
ele1.attribs["L"], ele1.attribs.lord_status, ele1.attribs.aperture_type
    # Access general attributes which are element-specific: ele1.attribs["L"], ele1.attribs.lord_status, ele1.attribs.aperture_type
    Out[10]:
    (0.0, 'Not_a_Lord', 'Rectangular')
    In [11]:
    Copied! 
    ele1.attribs
    ele1.attribs
    Out[11]:
    {'L': 0.0,
 'tilt': 0.0,
 'x_gain_err': 0.0,
 'y_gain_err': 0.0,
 'crunch': 0.0,
 'noise': 0.0,
 'osc_amplitude': 0.0,
 'x_gain_calib': 0.0,
 'y_gain_calib': 0.0,
 'crunch_calib': 0.0,
 'x_offset_calib': 0.0,
 'y_offset_calib': 0.0,
 'tilt_calib': 0.0,
 'de_eta_meas': 0.0,
 'n_sample': 0.0,
 'x_dispersion_err': 0.0,
 'y_dispersion_err': 0.0,

 'y_dispersion_calib': 0.0,
 'x_pitch': 0.0,
 'y_pitch': 0.0,
 'x_offset': 0.0,
 'y_offset': 0.0,
 'z_offset': 0.0,
 'delta_ref_time': 0.0,
 'p0c': 5288999975.314811,
 'e_tot': 5289000000.000001,
 'x_pitch_tot': 0.0,
 'y_pitch_tot': 0.0,
 'x_offset_tot': 0.0,
 'y_offset_tot': 0.0,
 'z_offset_tot': 0.0,
 'tilt_tot': 0.0,
 'ref_time_start': 0.0,
 'x1_limit': 0.0,
 'x2_limit': 0.0,
 'y1_limit': 0.0,
 'y2_limit': 0.0,
 'lord_status': 'Not_a_Lord',
 'slave_status': 'Free',
 'aperture_at': 'Exit_End',
 'offset_moves_aperture': False,
 'aperture_type': 'Rectangular'}
    In [12]:
    Copied! 
    # You can also view units and information on whether the attribute can be changed by using the following:
ele1.attrs["L"].units
    # You can also view units and information on whether the attribute can be changed by using the following: ele1.attrs["L"].units
    Out[12]:
    'm'

    Tao method commands¶

    For convenience, all of these commands are available as methods of the Tao class, and automatically parse the output.

    For example, to get the orbit at an s position:

    In [13]:
    Copied! 
    tao.orbit_at_s(s_offset=1.2)
    tao.orbit_at_s(s_offset=1.2)
    Out[13]:
    {'x': 0.00310868994973819,
 'px': 0.00344601640995989,
 'y': 0.00018321175239914,
 'py': 0.000248952325118158,
 'z': -0.000403684038665232,
 'pz': -7.17213865857442e-06,
 'spin': array([0., 0., 0.]),
 'field': array([0., 0.]),
 'phase': array([0., 0.]),
 's': 1.2,
 't': 4.00411570607715e-09,
 'charge': 0.0,
 'dt_ref': 0.0,
 'p0c': 5288999975.31481,
 'beta': 0.999999995332663,
 'ix_ele': 5,
 'state': 'Alive',
 'direction': 1,
 'species': 'Electron',
 'location': 'Inside'}

    Some commands return arrays:

    In [14]:
    Copied! 
    tao.evaluate("data::cbar.11[1:10]|model")
    tao.evaluate("data::cbar.11[1:10]|model")
    Out[14]:
    array([ 2.81129919e-03, -1.06243941e-03,  1.37712949e-04,  3.08052132e-04,
       -3.66410982e-04, -3.42737812e-04, -9.80307127e-06,  1.28290108e-03,
        2.66261121e-03,  2.68374458e-03])

    lat_list¶

    lat_list can be used to efficiently extract array data. By default this returns an array of floats:

    In [15]:
    Copied! 
    s = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", verbose=True)
s[0:5]
    s = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", verbose=True) s[0:5]
    pipe lat_list -array_out -track_only @>>*|model ele.s
    Out[15]:
    array([0.      , 0.      , 0.622301, 0.622301, 0.637956])

    These particulars keys will return integers:

    In [16]:
    Copied! 
    state = tao.lat_list("*", "orbit.state")
ix = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.ix_ele")
state.dtype, ix.dtype
    state = tao.lat_list("*", "orbit.state") ix = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.ix_ele") state.dtype, ix.dtype
    Out[16]:
    (dtype('<i4'), dtype('<i4'))

    And this one will return a list of strings:

    In [17]:
    Copied! 
    names = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.name")
names[0:5]
    names = tao.lat_list("*", "ele.name") names[0:5]
    Out[17]:
    ['BEGINNING', 'IP_L0', 'CLEO_SOL#3', 'DET_00W', 'CLEO_SOL#4']
    In [18]:
    Copied! 
    %config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    %config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina' import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

    Simple plot of this data

    In [19]:
    Copied! 
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s"), tao.lat_list("*", "orbit.vec.1"), marker=".");
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s"), tao.lat_list("*", "orbit.vec.1"), marker=".");

    Be careful with the flags. The defaults are:

    In [20]:
    Copied! 
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", flags="-array_out -track_only"));
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", flags="-array_out -track_only"));

    -index_order will return

    In [21]:
    Copied! 
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", flags="-array_out -index_order"));
    plt.plot(tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s", flags="-array_out -index_order"));

    All method commands¶

    There are many method commands. Please explore the documentation to find the ones that will best suit your needs.

    In [22]:
    Copied! 
    all_cmds = [name for name in dir(Tao) if not name.startswith("_")]
for cmd in all_cmds:
    print(cmd)
    all_cmds = [name for name in dir(Tao) if not name.startswith("_")] for cmd in all_cmds: print(cmd)

    There are many commands:

    In [23]:
    Copied! 
    len(all_cmds)
    len(all_cmds)
    Out[23]:
    145

    Each has documentation and an example associated with it:

    In [24]:
    Copied! 
    tao.data_d2?
    tao.data_d2?

    Other Tao instances¶

    Unfortunately there can only be one Tao instance per process, because the internal structures are held in memory and accessed via ctypes. So this will replace the current Tao session in memory.

    This looks like a new object:

    In [25]:
    Copied! 
    tao2 = Tao(
    init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True
)
    tao2 = Tao( init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True )

    but internally it's the same Tao instance:

    In [26]:
    Copied! 
    tao.lat_list("*", "ele.name")
    tao.lat_list("*", "ele.name")
    Out[26]:
    ['BEGINNING',
 'MAR.CSR',
 'FF.PIP00B',
 'FF.BEN01',
 'FF.PIP01',
 'FF.BEN02',
 'FF.PIP02A',
 'MAR.END',
 'END']

    SubprocessTao to the rescue¶

    Tao in a subprocess will work around this. You can then run as many Tao sessions as you like - just be sure to clean up your resources with .close_subprocess().

    In [27]:
    Copied! 
    from pytao import SubprocessTao

tao2 = SubprocessTao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True)
tao2.lat_list("*", "ele.name")
    from pytao import SubprocessTao tao2 = SubprocessTao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True) tao2.lat_list("*", "ele.name")
    Out[27]:
    ['BEGINNING',
 'MAR.CSR',
 'FF.PIP00B',
 'FF.BEN01',
 'FF.PIP01',
 'FF.BEN02',
 'FF.PIP02A',
 'MAR.END',
 'END']
    In [28]:
    Copied! 
    # Clean up
tao2.close_subprocess()
    # Clean up tao2.close_subprocess()

    Advanced users may also find that using SubprocessTao in a context manager is more intuitive:

    In [29]:
    Copied! 
    with SubprocessTao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True) as tao2:
    print(tao2.lat_list("*", "ele.name"))
    with SubprocessTao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/csr_beam_tracking/tao.init", noplot=True) as tao2: print(tao2.lat_list("*", "ele.name"))
    ['BEGINNING', 'MAR.CSR', 'FF.PIP00B', 'FF.BEN01', 'FF.PIP01', 'FF.BEN02', 'FF.PIP02A', 'MAR.END', 'END']

    Bunch data¶

    This example has bunch data. There are three methods:

    • tao.bunch_params to get a dict of statistical data

    • tao.bunch1 to get coordinate data arrays

    • tao.bunch_data to get a dict of many coordinate arrays. These can be used to instantiate a ParticleGroup object from the openPMD-beamphysics package.

    Statistical data:

    In [30]:
    Copied! 
    stats = tao.bunch_params("end")
stats.keys()
    stats = tao.bunch_params("end") stats.keys()
    Out[30]:
    dict_keys(['twiss_beta_x', 'twiss_alpha_x', 'twiss_gamma_x', 'twiss_phi_x', 'twiss_eta_x', 'twiss_deta_ds_x', 'twiss_etap_x', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_x', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_x', 'twiss_deta_dpz_x', 'twiss_detap_dpz_x', 'twiss_sigma_x', 'twiss_sigma_p_x', 'twiss_emit_x', 'twiss_norm_emit_x', 'twiss_beta_y', 'twiss_alpha_y', 'twiss_gamma_y', 'twiss_phi_y', 'twiss_eta_y', 'twiss_deta_ds_y', 'twiss_etap_y', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_y', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_y', 'twiss_deta_dpz_y', 'twiss_detap_dpz_y', 'twiss_sigma_y', 'twiss_sigma_p_y', 'twiss_emit_y', 'twiss_norm_emit_y', 'twiss_beta_z', 'twiss_alpha_z', 'twiss_gamma_z', 'twiss_phi_z', 'twiss_eta_z', 'twiss_deta_ds_z', 'twiss_etap_z', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_z', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_z', 'twiss_deta_dpz_z', 'twiss_detap_dpz_z', 'twiss_sigma_z', 'twiss_sigma_p_z', 'twiss_emit_z', 'twiss_norm_emit_z', 'twiss_beta_a', 'twiss_alpha_a', 'twiss_gamma_a', 'twiss_phi_a', 'twiss_eta_a', 'twiss_deta_ds_a', 'twiss_etap_a', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_a', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_a', 'twiss_deta_dpz_a', 'twiss_detap_dpz_a', 'twiss_sigma_a', 'twiss_sigma_p_a', 'twiss_emit_a', 'twiss_norm_emit_a', 'twiss_beta_b', 'twiss_alpha_b', 'twiss_gamma_b', 'twiss_phi_b', 'twiss_eta_b', 'twiss_deta_ds_b', 'twiss_etap_b', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_b', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_b', 'twiss_deta_dpz_b', 'twiss_detap_dpz_b', 'twiss_sigma_b', 'twiss_sigma_p_b', 'twiss_emit_b', 'twiss_norm_emit_b', 'twiss_beta_c', 'twiss_alpha_c', 'twiss_gamma_c', 'twiss_phi_c', 'twiss_eta_c', 'twiss_deta_ds_c', 'twiss_etap_c', 'twiss_dbeta_dpz_c', 'twiss_dalpha_dpz_c', 'twiss_deta_dpz_c', 'twiss_detap_dpz_c', 'twiss_sigma_c', 'twiss_sigma_p_c', 'twiss_emit_c', 'twiss_norm_emit_c', 'sigma_11', 'sigma_12', 'sigma_13', 'sigma_14', 'sigma_15', 'sigma_16', 'sigma_21', 'sigma_22', 'sigma_23', 'sigma_24', 'sigma_25', 'sigma_26', 'sigma_31', 'sigma_32', 'sigma_33', 'sigma_34', 'sigma_35', 'sigma_36', 'sigma_41', 'sigma_42', 'sigma_43', 'sigma_44', 'sigma_45', 'sigma_46', 'sigma_51', 'sigma_52', 'sigma_53', 'sigma_54', 'sigma_55', 'sigma_56', 'sigma_61', 'sigma_62', 'sigma_63', 'sigma_64', 'sigma_65', 'sigma_66', 'rel_min_1', 'rel_max_1', 'centroid_vec_1', 'rel_min_2', 'rel_max_2', 'centroid_vec_2', 'rel_min_3', 'rel_max_3', 'centroid_vec_3', 'rel_min_4', 'rel_max_4', 'centroid_vec_4', 'rel_min_5', 'rel_max_5', 'centroid_vec_5', 'rel_min_6', 'rel_max_6', 'centroid_vec_6', 'centroid_t', 'centroid_p0c', 'centroid_beta', 'ix_ele', 'direction', 'species', 'location', 's', 't', 'sigma_t', 'charge_live', 'n_particle_tot', 'n_particle_live', 'n_particle_lost_in_ele', 'beam_saved'])

    Array data:

    In [31]:
    Copied! 
    x = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="x")
px = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="px")
plt.scatter(x, px);
    x = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="x") px = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="px") plt.scatter(x, px);

    The state will be returned as an integer array.

    In [32]:
    Copied! 
    state = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="state")
state.dtype
    state = tao.bunch1("end", coordinate="state") state.dtype
    Out[32]:
    dtype('<i4')

    ParticleGroup from openPMD-beamphysics¶

    openPMD-beamphysics is an external package that can be useful for further bunch analysis, plotting, and conversion.

    https://github.com/ChristopherMayes/openPMD-beamphysics

    Here is example usage to extract bunch data as a ParticleGroup object.

    Note that the momentum units in openPMD-beamphysics are in eV/c, whereas Bmad's momenta are normalized by a reference momentum.

    In [33]:
    Copied! 
    P = tao.particles("end")
P.plot("x", "px")
    P = tao.particles("end") P.plot("x", "px")

    Tao's write format is already in the openPMD-beamphysics, so particles can be written and read in this way as well.

    In [34]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("write beam -at end test.h5")
    tao.cmd("write beam -at end test.h5")
    Out[34]:
    ['[INFO] tao_write_cmd:', '    Written: test.h5']
    In [35]:
    Copied! 
    from beamphysics import ParticleGroup

P2 = ParticleGroup("test.h5")
P2.plot("x", "px")
    from beamphysics import ParticleGroup P2 = ParticleGroup("test.h5") P2.plot("x", "px")
    In [36]:
    Copied! 
    # Cleanup
!rm test.h5
    # Cleanup !rm test.h5

    Error handling and Debugging¶

    All methods have a raises=True option. This will raise a RuntimeError if any errors are seen from Tao.

    In [37]:
    Copied! 
    tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s")
    tao.lat_list("*", "ele.s")
    Out[37]:
    array([0.   , 0.   , 0.06 , 0.193, 0.263, 0.385, 0.445, 0.445, 0.445])
    In [38]:
    Copied! 
    try:
    tao.var("foobar")
except Exception as ex:
    print("Exception handled:", ex)
    try: tao.var("foobar") except Exception as ex: print("Exception handled:", ex)
    Exception handled: Command: 'pipe var foobar ' causes errors in the function(s): tao_pipe_cmd

Error in tao_pipe_cmd:
  "pipe var foobar": Not a valid variable name

    This suppresses the exceptions, returning the error text:

    In [39]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("invalid_command", raises=False)
    tao.cmd("invalid_command", raises=False)
    Out[39]:
    ['[ERROR | 2026-APR-22 22:13:23] tao_command:',
 '    UNRECOGNIZED COMMAND: invalid_command']

    Logging¶

    All input commands are recorded as debug messages using standard Python logging.

    Enable stdout to see the log messages:

    In [40]:
    Copied! 
    import logging
import sys

logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.DEBUG)
    import logging import sys logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.DEBUG)
    In [41]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("sho ele 2");
    tao.cmd("sho ele 2");
    DEBUG:pytao.core:Tao> sho ele 2
    In [42]:
    Copied! 
    logging.getLogger("").setLevel(logging.INFO)
    logging.getLogger("").setLevel(logging.INFO)

    PyTao pipe internals¶

    Under the hood, PyTao uses the 'pipe' command that Tao offers in order to get structured data about your lattice back to Python. PyTao parses and makes a convenient format from this raw data, but you have the option of interacting with that raw data yourself by way of tao.cmd("pipe ...").

    In [43]:
    Copied! 
    tao = Tao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init", noplot=True)
    tao = Tao(init_file="$ACC_ROOT_DIR/bmad-doc/tao_examples/cesr/tao.init", noplot=True)
    In [44]:
    Copied! 
    %%tao
help pipe
    %%tao help pipe
    --------------
Tao> help pipe
The "pipe" command is like the "show" command in that the "pipe" command prints
information to the terminal. The difference is that the output from the "show" command is meant
for viewing by the user while the output of the "pipe" command is meant for easy
parsing. Format:
  pipe {-append <file_name>} {-noprint} <subcommand> <arguments>
  pipe {-write <file_name>} {-noprint} <subcommand> <arguments>

The "pipe" command has "-append" and "-write" optional arguments which can be used to
write the results to a file. The "pipe -append" command will appended to the output file. The
"pipe -write" command will first erase the contents of the output file. Example:
  pipe -write d2.dat data_d2    ! Write to file "d2.dat"

The "-noprint" option suppresses printing and is useful when writing large amounts of data to a
file.  The "pipe" command can be used to pass information to a parent process when Tao is run
as a subprocess.  The parent process may be any scripting program like Pipe, Perl, Tcl, etc.  In
particular, see the "Python Interface" chapter  for details on how to run
Tao as a Python subprocess.

In terms of long term maintainability, the advantage of using the "pipe" command in the scripts
over the "show" command comes from the fact that the output syntax of "show" commands can (and
does) change.

Note to programmers: For debugging, the "show internal -pipe" command will show the "c_real"
and "c_integer" arrays.

Possible "<subcommand>" choices are:
  beam, beam_init, branch1, bunch_comb, bunch_params, bunch1, bmad_com,
  building_wall_list, building_wall_graph, building_wall_point, building_wall_section,
  constraints, da_params, da_aperture, data, data_d2_create, data_d2_destroy,
  data_d_array, data_d1_array, data_d2, data_d2_array, data_set_design_value,
  data_parameter, datum_create, datum_has_ele, derivative,
  ele:ac_kicker, ele:cartesian_map, ele:chamber_wall, ele:control_var,
  ele:cylindrical_map, ele:elec_multipoles, ele:floor, ele:gen_attribs,
  ele:gen_grad_map, ele:grid_field, ele:head, ele:lord_slave, ele:mat6, ele:methods,
  ele:multipoles, ele:orbit, ele:param, ele:photon, ele:spin_taylor, ele:taylor,
  ele:twiss, ele:wake, ele:wall3d, em_field, enum, evaluate, floor_plan,
  floor_orbit, global, global:opti_de, global:optimization, global:ran_state,
  help, inum, lat_branch_list, lat_calc_done, lat_ele_list, lat_header, lat_list,
  lat_param_units, lord_control, matrix, merit, orbit_at_s, place_buffer,
  plot_curve, plot_curve_manage, plot_graph, plot_graph_manage, plot_histogram,
  plot_lat_layout, plot_line, plot_list, plot_symbol, plot_template_manage,
  plot_transfer, plot1, ptc_com, ring_general,
  shape_list, shape_manage, shape_pattern_list, shape_pattern_manage,
  shape_pattern_point_manage, shape_set, show, slave_control, space_charge_com,
  species_to_int, species_to_str, spin_invariant, spin_polarization, spin_resonance,
  super_universe, taylor_map, twiss_at_s, universe, var_v1_create, var_v1_destroy,
  var_create, var_general, var_v1_array, var_v_array, var, wall3d_radius, wave

When running Tao, to see documentation on any of the subcommands, use the command:
  help pipe <subcommand>
For example,
  help pipe ele:orbit
will show information on the "pipe ele:orbit" subcommand.

    This data is returned as specially formatted lists

    In [45]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("pipe orbit_at_s end")
    tao.cmd("pipe orbit_at_s end")
    Out[45]:
    ['x;REAL;F; -1.66698178808152E-05',
 'px;REAL;F;  2.38956895409344E-03',
 'y;REAL;F;  1.05487569250671E-06',
 'py;REAL;F;  1.77703588955956E-06',
 'z;REAL;F; -3.98929150181109E-04',
 'pz;REAL;F; -7.16337075709370E-06',
 'spin;REAL_ARR;F;  0.00000000000000E+00;  0.00000000000000E+00;  0.00000000000000E+00',
 'field;REAL_ARR;F;  0.00000000000000E+00;  0.00000000000000E+00',
 'phase;REAL_ARR;F;  9.01322977867567E-04;  0.00000000000000E+00',
 's;REAL;F;  7.68426421416168E+02',
 't;REAL;F;  2.56319599936047E-06',
 'charge;REAL;F;  0.00000000000000E+00',
 'dt_ref;REAL;F;  0.00000000000000E+00',
 'p0c;REAL;F;  5.28899997531481E+09',
 'beta;REAL;F;  9.99999995332664E-01',
 'ix_ele;INT;F;868',
 'state;STR;F;Alive',
 'direction;INT;F;1',
 'species;SPECIES;F;Electron',
 'location;STR;F;Downstream_End']

    Some commands have 'array_out' options. For example, this seems to return nothing:

    In [46]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd("pipe lat_list -array_out 1@0>>Q*|model orbit.floor.x")
    tao.cmd("pipe lat_list -array_out 1@0>>Q*|model orbit.floor.x")
    Out[46]:
    []

    But calling .cmd_real on the same command will get the data from an internal pointer:

    In [47]:
    Copied! 
    tao.cmd_real("pipe lat_list -array_out 1@0>>Q*|model orbit.floor.x")
    tao.cmd_real("pipe lat_list -array_out 1@0>>Q*|model orbit.floor.x")
    Out[47]:
    array([ 0.00000000e+00,  5.50520021e-03,  8.15067611e-03,  1.68511318e-02,
        1.30505799e-02, -1.28472902e-01, -6.17364478e-01, -1.63573061e+00,
       -3.15361765e+00, -4.96008406e+00, -8.44395025e+00, -1.25353254e+01,
       -1.53643143e+01, -1.93160730e+01, -2.35334231e+01, -2.86596006e+01,
       -3.40012284e+01, -4.11157702e+01, -4.73379456e+01, -5.39309817e+01,
       -6.08761276e+01, -6.66395280e+01, -7.38887367e+01, -8.14004769e+01,
       -8.91380411e+01, -9.70602496e+01, -1.07067452e+02, -1.15219118e+02,
       -1.23415239e+02, -1.31835984e+02, -1.39984607e+02, -1.48267474e+02,
       -1.57243532e+02, -1.65204340e+02, -1.72728166e+02, -1.80184448e+02,
       -1.85357659e+02, -1.92035947e+02, -2.00803298e+02, -2.06870809e+02,
       -2.12665460e+02, -2.18176438e+02, -2.23048889e+02, -2.27424213e+02,
       -2.31268354e+02, -2.34552354e+02, -2.35722781e+02, -2.38140772e+02,
       -2.39786180e+02, -2.41460795e+02, -2.42244501e+02, -2.42601929e+02,
       -2.42642700e+02, -2.42650644e+02, -2.42653710e+02, -2.42654861e+02,
       -2.42659287e+02, -2.42665723e+02, -2.42598208e+02, -2.42184673e+02,
       -2.41366879e+02, -2.39606396e+02, -2.37970180e+02, -2.35481963e+02,
       -2.34317757e+02, -2.30993043e+02, -2.27125234e+02, -2.22727557e+02,
       -2.17816005e+02, -2.12280141e+02, -2.06437553e+02, -2.00332913e+02,
       -1.91531574e+02, -1.84820353e+02, -1.79653411e+02, -1.72176810e+02,

       -1.31238483e+02, -1.22815238e+02, -1.14621367e+02, -1.06478607e+02,
       -9.64783788e+01, -8.85696832e+01, -8.08418076e+01, -7.33402391e+01,

       -1.88775802e+01, -1.50782581e+01, -1.22578099e+01, -8.16226154e+00,

       -5.55550939e-03, -1.53850510e-03])

    Cleanup¶

    In [48]:
    Copied! 
    !rm -f csr_wake.dat
    !rm -f csr_wake.dat
